data.title: Boeing Is Flying Steady Into 2026
data.description:
Boeing's (BA) stock in the last 12 months is up 38.9% while the S&P is up 14.4%. While this is a very good one-year return, there is a lot more to this story than meets the eye.

Boeing obviously has had numerous issues in recent years that they have had to overcome. Mass loss of lives from planes going down, claims of sub-par materials and poor quality control, along with a door flying off in mid-air over Portland shortly after take off, have greatly damaged their reputation not only with air travelers, but with investors in the stocks.

In other words, it has been a very rocky decade for the company, which has also led to a very volatile ride for shareholders in the company. In this article, we will take a look at the past and recent performance of the shares and then do a five-year valuation going forward based on the numbers that are out there on the street right now.

This is the same process that I put all stocks through in my very demanding and stringent investment stock selection process.

On my Tuesday, Jan. 26, edition of my daily radio show/podcast, 'Best Stocks Now', I discussed whether or not Boeing (BA) is worthy of a spot amongst the 22 other stocks that we currently own in our premier growth portfolio. This is one of four portfolios that I manage and mirror here in Seeking Alpha with my premium service.

The other three portfolios are Ultra Growth, Emerging Growth, and Value/Relative Value.

Our cash position right now in the Premier Growth portfolio is currently at about 25%. This is not because we are bearish, but because we are picky! I currently have 7-8 vacant slots to finish filling up this 7 year old portfolio. Does Boeing currently meet my very stringent criteria?

Boeing, one of the thirty stocks in the Dow Jones Industrial Average, reported their quarterly earnings on Tuesday. As you can see from the chart below, this is one of their best quarters in a long, long time.

BA's earnings in Q4 look great on paper, with $9.92 per share in earnings and a 57% jump in sales. But, one of the reasons for that explosion of profit, or at least a large part of it, is that it sold a big piece of its business.

On April 22nd of last year, 2025, Boeing sold part of its Digital Aviation Solutions unit. That sale alone created a one-time profit of $9.6 billion. This is the main reason that Q4 looks so good on paper.

Now, that's not to say the core business isn't getting better. Even ignoring the one-time sale, Boeing delivered 160 airplanes this quarter. That is 103 more than last year. Their revenue jumped 57% year over year, and their cash flow has finally turned positive once again.

However, Boeing still has some real issues to work through. Their commercial airplane business might be full, but they're still losing money. Defense programs, especially tankers, had losses, and Boeing still carries $54 billion in debt!

Despite the issues though, Boeing is healing. In August 2024, they replaced long-time CEO Dave Calhoun with Kelly Ortberg. While Calhoun had led Boeing through a very turbulent time filled with pandemic impacts, intensified regulatory scrutiny, and an abundance of 737 MAX safety issues; the culture of Boeing under his leadership was less connected to the reality of manufacturing and more driven towards temporary, often dangerous, gains.

Ortberg, however, has emphasized engineering excellence, quality control, and operational stability over short-term earnings. The shift towards a new hands-on engineering and operations-focused Boeing can be seen quite literally, as the CEO's office was relocated back to the Boeing production heartland of Seattle.

Now for some basic facts about the company

Data from Best Stock Now App

Boeing currently has a market-cap of almost $195 billion. It is a large-cap stock with a moderate to conservative risk profile (although it has carried a lot more risk than that in recent years Also, keep in mind that they still have a lot of liability out there to work their way through.

My stock analysis process always begins with looking at the track record of the shares and comparing it against the other 5,193 stocks, ETFs, and mutual funds in my proprietary database.

Data from Best Stocks Now App

As you can see from the screenshot above BA's stock is up 38.9% over the last twelve months, while the S&P is up 14.4%. The stock has delivered some alpha lately, but we need to look at this short-term performance in context with its longer-term track record.

The stock has delivered an average annual total return of just 4.76% over the last three years that compares with an average annual return of 24% per year for the S&P 500. That meager return has come along with a lot of volatility and a lot of risk.

Over the last five years, the stock has delivered an average annual total return of just 3.89% vs. an average annual return of 16.3% for the S&P 500. Over the last ten years, the stock has rewarded investors with an average annual return of 7.86% while the much less volatile S&P 500 has returned an outsized average of 26.7%.

What a decade for the market it has been! At the same time, it has been a very volatile and weak decade for Boeing.

But that's all looking in the rear-view mirror. When I'm deciding what stocks to put into my portfolios, I also always look at a five-year forward valuation. That is looking forward. This valuation is critical to me, and honestly the Seattle-based company's current valuation doesn't look like it's making it to the Super Bowl like the Seahawks are this time around.

In my 5 year valuation, my earnings growth rate estimate for BA is 8%, now, that's a little bit higher than the consensus estimate of 5%; In my opinion however, their impressive backlog deserves a higher estimate of growth than 5%.

After extrapolating those earnings out over the next five years and then applying a multiple of 45, due to their backlog as well as me being generous, I am led to a target price of $349.33 in 5 years. A horribly subpar value that represents just 43% upside potential. I require 80% or more for the stock to be considered for my portfolios, and with where Boeing is today, they just aren't cutting it.

As a professional money manager over the last 25 years, I have looked at a lot of stock charts. I currently look at about 500 one-year charts per day. Are they in an uptrend, sideways trend, downtrend, or a leveling off trend? Are they breaking out or breaking down? Where are the current support and resistance lines?

Boeing has been in an uptrend that looks to be leveling off. Support is at $175, and resistance is at $250. I would call this a neutral chart at the current time.

In my Best Stocks Now app, BA is currently rated 1,759 out of 5,192 and is sitting at a Value grade of F and a Performance grade of C-, and that is just not good enough for a spot in our portfolio.

If you already own stock in BA, I would recommend a cautious but optimistic HOLD. Boeing has its largest backlog in history currently, and has no real competitors outside of Airbus (EADSF).

Ultimately, though, it's just not looking good enough for me to buy at the current time. While I am happy with the 22 positions that I currently own in this portfolio, I am still looking for seven more positions to fill it up, however. Any suggestions?

data.title: Honeywell Q4 Earnings Review: Margin Expansion Is Just Beginning
data.description:
Reviewing Honeywell's Earnings Results

Honeywell International Inc. (HON), a $138-billion market cap conglomerate, reported its Q4 earnings update yesterday. They basically beat only on the adjusted EPS (by 2.14%), missing the revenue consensus by 1.62% or ~$161 million (that was the first miss since mid-2024). The portfolio transformations and the costs of spinning off businesses in 2026 have mostly led to negative revisions on both the top and bottom lines (especially for the nearest Q1 2026 quarter):

But the stock price closed 4.89% higher after the print. Why?

JPM analysts wrote that HON's messy 2026 EPS profile will be cleared with the guide, creating an opportunity ahead of the looming break-up, on which the bank expects to get more clarity on many key fronts in the next 6 months. That's the reason for their upgrade and the rise of PT.

What the market seemingly decided to focus on, in addition to just the headline figures, was HON's massive 23% surge in organic orders and a record backlog exceeding $37 billion.

Particularly in long-cycle sectors like Aerospace and Building Automation, we finally got more sales growth visibility thanks to the trends in backlog. Also, the Aerospace spin-off timeline was pulled forward to Q3 2026 - that was disclosed at the earnings call. It told the market participants that HON has been progressing faster than was initially expected.

Now specifically coming to the aerospace question, right now, I will admit that we are so heavily focused to make spin happen in Q3, we'll share the specifics of stranded costs, et cetera, during our Investor Day coming up in June. But we are absolutely confident and committed that we will eliminate stranded costs in 12 to 18 months' time. Earlier, the better, we absolutely get it. But that's the range we expect to take it out.

Source: HON's Q4 Earnings call

In addition to that, the "real operating metrics" like organic sales growth of 11% and especially FCF trends (+48% YoY) were quite healthy in Q4, so it couldn't be ignored.

The guidance for FY2026 appears to be constructive, with a revenue range expected at $38.8-39.8 billion (that's ~$280 million below Wall Street's $39.58 billion consensus view), implying an organic growth of just 3-7%. During transitional periods like the one HON is going through now, it's quite normal, in my opinion. On the positive side, the profitability should generally improve as HON's management targets segment margins expanding 20 to 60 bps to a 22.7-23.1% range in FY2026. Effectively, that should be enough to offset the 30-basis-point headwind from an incremental $100 million investment in HON's Quantinuum unit.

The firm's consolidated FCF generation should stay at solid levels and expand by 4-10% YoY in FY2026, which will provide enough liquidity for further debt reduction before the spin-off in Q3 2026.

Honewell's Underappreciated Future

By the second half of this year, HON won't be a four-segment behemoth anymore as its Aerospace Technologies division will spin off and start to trade under the ticker (HONA) by Q3. In addition to that, they'll complete the separation of their Automation business. As a result, the market will face 3 standalone industry leaders: Honeywell Aerospace, Honeywell Automation, and the already-spun Solstice Advanced Materials.

The main positive from that move should be seen in the PostCo's financials. After exiting the loss-making and lower-margin Productivity Solutions and Services (PSS) and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions (WWS), Honeywell should have better margins in 2027-2028 - at least that's my understanding. So far, the market is failing to reflect that - after the faster-than-expected progress on the spin-off that we saw in Q4, the EPS revisions for 2027-2030 haven't really changed for the better.

One more thing that might positively impact HON's margins by 2027 is Honeywell's plans to make Quantinuum, its 54%-owned quantum unit, a public entity (likely by the end of 2026).

If they get the $10 billion expected valuation for it, it's going to unlock ~$5.4 billion in value for HON, and they'll be able to deleverage faster (thus, reducing the interest payments and boosting the EPS growth). From the current consensus estimates, that's not seen yet.

Another underappreciated catalyst that might materialize after the business separation is the firm's pivot toward AI-enabled autonomous solutions. HON has a deep domain experience and a vast installed base to drive its industrial transition from automation to autonomy relatively quickly. With their strategic partnership with Google (GOOG) that started in 2024, they can now integrate next-generation AI models and systems into industrial data to streamline autonomous operations.

With more AI-driven processes/operations, the margins should naturally pick up throughout the next few years, in my understanding.

Valuation Promises Some Upside Potential

I believe with a simplified corporate structure, HON shareholders can better capitalize on the existing "valuation gap" with some similar-sized peers such as RTX Corporation (RTX). JPM's analysts wrote in their update on HON that the currently visible disconnect should narrow thanks to its world-class aerospace business, with an absence of the "conglomerate discount".

I don't expect the gap to fade next year, though.

If I put a historically normal premium on the FY2027 EPS growth projection of 0.50%, and assume that HON adjusts toward a 24-25x earnings multiple by that time, I'll get a 12-month price target of ~$282.67/share.

$11.48 * 1.005 * 24.5x = $282.67/share (+24.39% upside)

Source: The author's calculations

With that in mind, I think HON deserves a "Buy" rating.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

It's always quite risky to buy companies in the middle of their business transition cycles because one can't be sure that the execution will be intact. So far, I like HON's developments on the separation front. However, I agree with the skeptics that some dissynergies might emerge that the bulls don't even think exist. Plus, there will be additional costs that may take years to prove they were actually reasonably spent.

Some of the catalysts and themes that I believe should help HON's rally in 2026 may turn out to be insignificant. For example, let's take the Quantinuum prospective IPO. We can't know if it goes public in 2026 (or in 2027-2028). It's currently running on "hype" with widening losses due to massive R&D and capital burn, so investors might require a cut to the expected valuation of $10 billion. For FY2026, the management plans an incremental $100 million YoY increase in investment for Quantinuum, and these capital injections may not lead to any profitable results for shareholders in the foreseeable future.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, I like HON's value proposition after the business separation. They're not just breaking down businesses to get cash and pay it out to shareholders - I see great logic behind the latest corporate steps. In my opinion, HON should eventually close the gap with RTX's valuation. If that happens, the upside potential from the current stock price might exceed 24%. Based on all that, I decided to rate HON as a "Buy".

Thank you for reading!

data.title: New Oriental Education: A 'Buy' With Beat And Raise Quarter (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become a bull on New Oriental Education (EDU) (9901.HK).

The company's 2QFY2026 (YE May 31) net income exceeded the market's expectations by a wide margin. It also revised the full year's sales guidance upwards.

EDU is still buying back its own shares. That's supportive of a potential 5+% capital management yield (cash returned/market cap).

Its 4QFY25 outperformance and worse-than-anticipated FY26 outlook were detailed in my prior July 31, 2025 update.

I'm Positively Surprised By Q2 Earnings

The firm released its recent three-month results on Wednesday, Jan 28.

EDU's Sep-Nov '25 normalized bottom-line was +69% higher year-on-year at $73M. That's also 31% ahead of the sell-side's projection as per S&P Capital IQ.

A faster pace of growth and enhanced profitability contributed to its strong showing.

The company's YoY top-line increase improved from 1QFY26's +6% to 2QFY26's +15%. The absolute figure of $1.19B was above the consensus's $1,163M estimate. Note that the split by business isn't disclosed.

Its adult/university- and college-related divisions' revenues rose 13% and 14%, respectively in the latest quarter. But the "New Educational Business Initiatives/NEBI" segment was the clear stand-out.

EDU highlighted at its Q2 briefing that "student enrollment" and "retention rates" for NEBI's "non-academic tutoring" operations benefited from "better word of mouth." I think that the optimization of user acquisition tactics was successful. This unit's 2QFY2026 turnover went up +22%.

Separately, the group's EBIT-to-sales widened by +4.7ppts (percentage points) YoY to 7.5% for this fiscal year's 2nd quarter. This is 200bps (basis points) ahead of the analysts' expectations.

You would have thought that EDU needed more advertising spend and aggressive capacity build-up to do well. That wasn't the case.

Its earnings call commentary indicated that "we slowed down the learning center expansion from 20%, 30% the year before" to FY26's targeted +10%. Also, 2QFY26 "Selling & Marketing/S&M" costs amounting to $0.19B represented a 1% contraction on YoY terms.

Management Is More Upbeat About Full-Year Prospects Now

The mid-point of EDU's expected FY26 turnover growth has been lifted from +7.5% to +10.0%.

In the preceding section, I outlined the solid top-line performances of its NEBI, adult-university, and college units.

The only weak spots in Q2 were "Overseas Test Preparation/OTP" and "Overseas Study Consulting Services/OSCS." Both of them delivered a low-single digit YoY sales increase during this period.

But I'm predicting a turnaround in 2HFY26.

The enterprise shared at the results meeting that "we (recently) merged the overseas test prep and consulting business."

Chinese students with study plans abroad will have to take English-language examinations and also seek advice on other admission-related matters. You can imagine that EDU wasn't able to meet their needs effectively by operating OSCS and OTP separately in the past.

Going forward, I think the firm can hold on to clients for longer by using initial test-prep solutions to cross-sell ongoing advisory services. That should translate into higher overseas-related educational revenues.

Also, EDU's analyst briefing remarks also stressed that "we are quite optimistic about the margin expansion in the second half of this year." The consensus FY2026 EBIT-to-revenue forecast is 12.4%, implying a 110bps improvement.

I believe the OTP-OSCS combination is likely to lead to a decrease in labor expenses. This is evidenced by the CFO's 2Q call comment that "one person can do more jobs even stronger than before."

Eyes On Mid-Single Digit Stockholder Yield

EDU is making healthy progress with its existing $0.3B buyback plan. That's in effect for a year beginning in Oct '25.

It spent $86M to purchase 1.6M of its "American Depositary Shares/ADS" in the last three months. It's equivalent to almost three-tenths of the entire program. This gives me confidence that the stock can realize a forward buyback-to-market cap of 3.2%.

The group has also committed to a cash payout of $1.2/ADS for the current fiscal year. It implies a 2.0% dividend-to-price ratio.

In my opinion, EDU's potential shareholder yield of 5.2% (2.0+3.2) will help to attract income-focused investors.

You Should Contemplate These Bear-Case Scenarios

The firm's NEBI division might cede ground to rivals, if its "word-of-mouth" marketing approach is executed poorly.

Demand for its OSCS/OTP offerings could weaken in the future. This assumes interest in foreign studies wanes.

The company may fail to complete its repurchase program. A diversion of capital to M&As is a possibility.

The Final Word

I'm changing my rating for this name from 'Hold' to 'Buy'.

EDU trades at a next twelve months' P/E of 17x. The same metric for its listed comparable TAL Education (TAL) is 23 times.

The stock boasts an upside of +35% (23/17). I've identified re-rating triggers such as sustained buybacks and the achievement of its full-year guide.

data.title: GDXJ: Gold Miners Are Making Massive Profits, With Prospects Of Strong Returns In 2026
data.description:
An unprecedented surge in gold prices has enabled miners to make gigantic profits last year. A blistering profit growth has also been translating into substantial returns for shareholders. VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) delivered a price return of 203% in the last twelve months with 137% increase in dividend payments. Given the strong demand for gold and other precious metals, I believe the uptrend is likely to continue in 2026. Therefore, I initiate coverage of GDJX with a buy rating.

Gold Price Surge is Likely to Continue in 2026

After generating a whopping 62% price increase in the previous year, Gold prices kicked off 2026 with a solid surge of more than 20% in the first month of the year. The precious metal achieved a record high of $5300 per ounce. The market pundits expect gold to mark new records in fiscal 2026 with bullish year end forecasts hovering in a range of $6,000 per ounce. A continued demand for the precious metal is likely to be fuelled by central banks aggressive buying, waning interest in US dollar, and geopolitical tensions.

In particular, China is playing a key role in pushing the prices higher amid its strategy of increasing gold reserves while lowering its dependence on US dollar. As of the end of 2025, the People's Bank of China accumulated 74.14 million fine troy ounce of gold. The bank’s gold buying increased in the past 14 consecutive months.

Similarly, other central banks are also seeking to increase their gold reserves. For example, as of the end of 2025, Poland's central bank’s gold reserves were around 550 tons. The bank’s Governor Adam Glapinski plans to boost gold reserves to 700 tons.

Besides the central banks gold buying strategy, the geopolitical tensions have also been adding to the precious metal’s demand. Increasing tensions between US and Europe over Greenland, the potential war with Iran and continued risk of tariffs are adding to safe-haven demand.

Whereas, Silver, which Goldman Sachs analyst believes is behaving like 'gold squared' or 'gold on steroids, rallied 240% in the last twelve months. The analyst further anticipates that this trend is likely extend until silver attains its historical standards compared to the gold. Silver’s price rally was supported speculative interest and strong physical demand. Overall, gold and silver market fundamentals appear strong to extend the uptrend in fiscal 2026.

It’s Time to Capitalize on Gold’s Price Growth

As the gold prices are rallying, it is a great time to capitalize on the potential uptrend. One of the best ways to make the most out of the precious metal’s upside is by investing directly in mining companies. This strategy can also enable investors to capitalize on silver’s upside because several gold companies are also involved in silver mining.

However, a single-stock investment carries a higher risk factor. Therefore, considering gold-focused ETFs can help investors to lower a risk factor while capitalizing on the broader market trends.

VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF could be a worth conditioning investment vehicle. GDXJ's share price appreciated around 203% in the last twelve months, significantly higher than Gold’s spot price surge of 97%. Its price surge also outshined prominent large-cap gold-focused ETFs, including SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and iShares Gold Trust (IAU).

GDXJ’s blistering price performance is supported by its exposure to both gold and silver mining companies. In addition, investors growing interest in small-caps in easing market conditions is contributing to its price upside. A low interest rate increases small caps potential to boost their investments in growth opportunities.

GDXJ’s portfolio comprises 96 stocks with the top 10 making up 42% of its portfolio weight. All of its top 10 holdings experienced a sharp share price surge in the last twelve months amid the robust price trend and profitability growth in the precious metals markets.

For example, Pan American Silver Corp (PAAS), GDXJ’s largest stock holding, experienced a share price surge of more than 180% in the last twelve months. The company’s earnings per share is expected to increase more than 170% year over year in 2025. Its earnings per share is also forecasted to grow by 60% in fiscal 2026. Whereas, Coeur Mining’s (CDE) share price rallied 286% in the last twelve months amid its substantial earnings growth power. The company is likely to deliver 409% earnings growth in 2025 and 90% in 2026.

Alamos Gold (AGI), Equinox Gold Corp (EQX) and Royal Gold (RGLD) are also among GDXJ’s top five holdings. Alamos Gold is expected to deliver 65% earnings growth in 2025 and 87% in 2026. The company’s stock price rallied 105% in the last twelve months. Equinox Gold has also made several financial and production records in the latest reported quarter. The company stated that with the ramp up of its Greenstone and Valentine assets, its production is likely to increase in 2026.

Royal Gold expects more than 50% year over year earnings growth in fiscal 2025 and 2026. The company’s share price rallied 110% in the last twelve months. In the latest reported quarter, gold contributed 78% to its record revenue of $252 million while silver and copper represented 12% and 7%.

The rest of GDXJ’s top 10 holdings include Hecla Mining (HL), First Majestic Silver Corp (AG), Evolution Mining (CAHPF) and Iamgold Corp (IAG). All these companies also reported a blistering share price surge in the last twelve months due to their massive revenue and earnings growth power. For example, Hecla Mining’s share price surged almost 380% in the last twelve months while First Majestic Silver Corp, and Iamgold Corp experienced a price increase of 354% and 248%, respectively.

GDXJ Characteristics

In addition to a solid price surge, VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF offers a solid dividend yield of nearly 1.76%. GDXJ lifted its dividend by a massive 137% in the last twelve months. It has increased its dividends in the last three consecutive years. Dividend return contributed 7% to its total return of 210% in the last twelve months. GDXJ’s dividend growth prospects are high for 2026 as gold and silver companies are poised to generate a significant profit growth.

Moreover, despite a strong share price surge, GDXJ's trailing price to earnings ratio of 27 is in-line with the S&P 500. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.51% is slightly high compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. Moreover, GDXJ’s liquidity is healthy with more than 5.7 million in average shares trading volume in the past three months.

Risk Factors to Consider

Gold is one of the most volatile precious metals with a strong correlation to the broader economic, political and monetary trends. Geopolitical stability and sustainable economic growth can negatively impact its price. The central banks higher concentration on the US dollar could also hurt gold's performance. A strong stock market bull run can also negatively impact safe-heaven assets. GDXJ’s standard deviation is around 35 and annualized price volatility is above 40%. Both metrics are up significantly from the median of all ETFs.

In Conclusion

Gold and other precious metals are expected to be the top performers in 2026, given geopolitical conflicts and economic uncertainty. The central banks across the world are also expected to boost their gold reserves. From an investment prospective, investment vehicles, such as VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF, could enable investors make the most out of the uptrend. GDXJ generated blistering returns in 2025 due to its portfolio exposure to mid and small cap gold and silver companies from across the world.

data.title: Buy The Molina Dip: CMS Proposes Flat Medicare Advantage Increases For 2027
data.description:
Introduction

I first wrote about Molina Healthcare (MOH) in December 2025 highlighting the business dynamics and why I see the company as a strong investment opportunity amid weak market sentiment - you can read that article here. The reason for revisiting Molina is due to the sell-off across sector this week after the Centres for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) proposed lower than expected rate increases for Medicare Advantage (MA) plans in 2027. CMS's final ruling is expected in April, analysts had been expecting a ~5% rate increase while the proposal is for 0.09% (essentially flat).

Many investors bought the healthcare insurance dip in 2025, after higher utilisation, climbing medical costs and falling profitability. Investors expect 2026 to be an adjustment year, due to rapid rate increases across various programmes. However, this news relating to government programs like Medicare caused a sell-off across the sector. The message to the market - rate increases aren't as high as you expected.

Rhetoric from Trump over the years implies a very strong stance against health insurers and pharmaceutical giants alike. Overall my thesis on Molina remains mostly unchanged. Molina's Medicare exposure is 266,000 patients as of Q3, equivalent to 4.7% of their total plan members.

At the segment level, Medicare accounts for 14.5% of premium revenue and 14.6% of medical margin profit for the 9 months ending September 30th.

This is a relatively small segment and thus Molina's profits will be mostly protected with maybe some slight margin pressure at group level if the 0.09% 2027 rate increase gets approved. Overall I believe the core thesis (higher medicaid rates = lower group MCR = profit recovery) remains in tack. If the proposed MA rate changes stand, MCR for Molina's Medicare business will climb in 2027 but as a smaller segment impact is limited.

Sector Reaction

UnitedHealth (UNH) took the largest hit opening down around 19% intraday on the 27th of January, while other health insurers saw less extreme drops including Molina which is currently down 4.6% this week.

The market’s reaction appears more sentiment driven than fundamental, reflecting a broad reassessment of regulatory risk across all government-exposed insurers rather than a Molina-specific deterioration. The health insurance sector as a whole has taken hit after hit over the past 12 months, but the health insurance business model is not structurally broken.

The main concern of the street and the market is what happens if the U.S. government becomes increasingly hostile towards health insures (even the less aggressive coders like - Molina) by refusing justified rate increases across medicaid. Medicaid plans is the bulk of Molina's business and capitation-rate increases are set and agreed independently with each state annually.

Premiums must be actuarially sound as defined by CMS who can approve or reject capitation rate changes. These are done annually (different times for each state) and premiums lag as pricing for 2026 is based on 2025/24 data. Ultimately, if states propose rates which results in insurers being underpaid, insurers will pull out of managed care programs thereby deteriorating healthcare coverage. State representatives would being exposing themselves to backlash in this unlikely scenario.

From a structural system standpoint, insurers in medicaid are playing catch-up and operators like Molina are highly likely to experience profit recovery over the coming 24 months.

Valuation Snapshot

Let's assume the market is willing to pay a 8% FCF yield for Molina. In other words the company could be trading at a market cap to FCF of 12.5x, which reflects the volatility in earnings and positive drift of claims inflation which leads to higher premiums (higher revenue = higher profits long term). A multiple in the low teens is justifiable, given the high-barriers to entry and modest growth rate.

Ignoring Molina's negative net debt for a minute, just using sell-side FCF estimates and a valuation exercise (12.5x FCF), we can see the intrinsic value per share rises dramatically over the coming years. In the example above I'm using a current share price of $187. This assumes zero buybacks which management can comfortably do given their cash war chest of $8.45bn (debt is only $3.66bn). Management repurchased 2.8 million shares in Q3 utilising $500m of the $1bn buyback programme which runs until December 31st 2026. Management have likely drawn a portion of the remaining $500m authorisation in Q4 and into Q1.

If the sell-off caused by the proposed MA 2027 rate changes continues, management will probably seize the opportunity with more buybacks. At current levels another $1bn buyback programme (11.8% of cash), represents 5.32 million shares or 10% of shares outstanding.

Conclusion

The negative sentiment and regulatory uncertainty across U.S. health insurers has created what I believe is a meaningful mis-pricing in Molina shares. Molina shares are up 25% from my last article and I maintain my view that Molina is a "strong buy". While the proposed Medicare Advantage rate update for 2027 has understandably unsettled investors, its direct impact on Molina is limited given the company’s relatively small exposure to Medicare Advantage and its much larger reliance on Medicaid, where rate increases are negotiated at the state level.

This does not imply an immediate turnaround in 2026. Rather, Molina’s investment case rests on a gradual normalisation of medical cost ratios as Medicaid rate resets catch up with elevated utilisation. The key signal for investors over the next 12–18 months will be whether Molina can demonstrate improving Medicaid margins (MCR) as state pricing adjusts.

The proposed MA rate changes for 2027 is a small concern, investors will have to wait to see what CMS finalises in April. Last year CMS changed their MA price proposals from an initial 2.23% to 5.06%, more than double.

Therefore there is upside optionality if CMS revises Medicare Advantage rates higher in April, as it did last year, but even if they don't it doesn't break the Molina thesis. Medicare Advantage is a small area of the Molina group, which is highly exposed to Medicaid (75% of revenue) where rate increases are negotiated at the state-level. Although it would be good to see both ACA and Medicare lines perform well as this would translate to additional upside for investors.

data.title: Spain's Economy Ends 2025 With A Bang
data.description:
By Ruben Dewitte, Economist

A strong finish to 2025

The Spanish economy grew by 0.8% quarter-on-quarter in the final months of 2025, an acceleration from the 0.6% QoQ growth in the third quarter, and beating the consensus by 0.2 percentage points.

The figure was hot enough to briefly overwhelm the servers of the Spanish statistical office this morning. Jokes aside, a small downward revision to first-quarter figures nudged full‑year growth to 2.6%, slightly below previous forecasts. But the key message remains: Spain entered 2026 with solid momentum.

Growth drivers point to trend continuation

In terms of growth drivers, the established pattern continued. Household consumption rose by a solid 1.0% QoQ for the third consecutive quarter, while investment increased by 1.7%, supported once again by a strong surge in intellectual property investment (+2.7% QoQ). Government consumption was broadly flat, and net exports remained a drag on growth amid a challenging global environment.

From a production perspective, all major sectors posted positive growth, albeit with diverging trends. Manufacturing output slowed for the second consecutive quarter, expanding by just 0.1% QoQ. According to the S&P Global PMIs, declining output and shrinking order books under competitive pressure explain this weakness.

The evolution also contrasts with the improvement seen in yesterday’s economic sentiment indicators, which seem to understate the pronounced decline in reported export orders. Services, by contrast, continued to grow robustly, even as signs of a tourism slowdown became more apparent. After several years of exceptional performance, we expect growth in the tourism sector to normalise.

Growth normalisation remains the base case for 2026

The same forces that shaped the end of 2025 will continue to determine Spain’s growth outlook in 2026. Government consumption is expected to make only a limited contribution in the absence of a new budget. Private consumption is expected to gradually normalise after several strong quarters, and net exports are likely to remain subdued, partly because of the stronger euro. The real effective exchange rate (REER) of the euro has risen 6.1% since January 2025, which appears to be weighing on external demand.

Against this backdrop, investment will be the key variable in Spain in 2026. As noted earlier, much of Spain’s recent growth has been quantitative, driven by substantial migration inflows that expanded the labour force. The government’s recent regularisation plan that expects to grant legal status to around 500,000 people, equivalent to roughly 2% of the current legal labour force, suggests a continuation of this strategy. However, while regularisation may yield important social and labour market benefits, its macroeconomic impact is likely to be more limited than previous immigration‑led expansions of labour supply.

Meanwhile, productivity per hour worked declined again in Q4 2025, underscoring the need for productivity‑enhancing investments if Spain is to shift toward more structural and sustainable growth. A significant source of upside on this front comes from the EU Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF). Spain still has about €20bn in RRF grants to disburse by the end of 2026, equivalent to roughly 6% of annual investment spending. With capacity utilisation rising to 79.8% in Q4 2025, conditions for private investment are also favourable. Although the productivity effects of these investments may not be immediate, they could mark a gradual transition toward higher quality, more productive growth, helping Spain maintain its outperformance relative to other eurozone economies in 2026 and beyond.

Overall, we have revised our 2026 growth forecast upward to 2.4%, though the upgrade is driven primarily by the strong carry‑over from Q4 2025. We do not foresee meaningful changes in the underlying quarterly growth profile.

Inflation to follow the normalisation trend

Meanwhile, January’s inflation data came in slightly hotter than expected at 2.5% year‑on‑year, though still 0.5 percentage points lower than December 2025 thanks to a 0.7% month‑on‑month decline in prices. The drop was driven mainly by a more moderate increase in electricity prices compared with last year. As such, the data point to a continued normalisation of Spain’s inflation profile, in line with the broader normalisation of the economy.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: The Coca-Cola Company: Slowing Spending By Lower-Income Individuals Is A Significant Red Flag
data.description:
Investing is often more than the simple and usually straightforward metrics most use to value a company, the story being told almost always matters as well. While most corporations miss earnings expectations occasionally, when the narrative that supports the value of a growth investment breaks, investors usually have a hard time regaining confidence in the company.

The Coca-Cola Company (KO), founded in 1892, is one of the oldest and most well-known brands in the world. The beverage seller originally was predominantly a US operator, but today the more mature company gets nearly 60 percent of the corporation's revenues from outside of the United States. The company has also seen growth slow significantly in recent years.

The Coca-Cola Company has offered investors total returns of 32.16 percent over the last 3 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 78.34 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about this iconic company in August of 2025. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating. I wanted to update my coverage of the beverage seller since the company reported third-quarter earnings and management is set to disclose fourth-quarter earnings on February 10th.While the Coca-Cola company has recently benefited from a weak dollar in the forex markets, the company continues to struggle to achieve real or meaningful volume sales growth with core products in the main markets the beverage seller operates in. The recent currency moves the company benefited from are not likely to be sustainable either. The company looks overvalued.

The Coca-Cola company's third-quarter earnings report slightly beat expectations, but a more detailed analysis of this statement should concern investors for several reasons. Management reported that the company's earnings per share normalized actual was $.82, while the beverage seller stated revenue actual was $12.41 billion. This report slightly beat expectations for EPS normalized actual of $.82 a share, and revenue actual of $12.42 billion. Management also reiterated previous full-year guidance of comparable earnings per share growth of 3 percent, and organic revenue to increase by 5-6 percent.

The most important metric for the Coca-Cola company is volume sales of the core soda brand in the most important markets the corporation operates within.The beverage seller still getsnearly 45 percent of overall revenues from sales of the Coca-Cola beverage and a concentrate version of the product.

Investors should be concerned about the fact that the company reported flat volume case unit growth in the third quarter within the US market, and volume case unit growth for the last 9 months has been negative 1 percent.

Similarly, management stated that volume case unit growth in Latin America was also flat for the quarter and is negative 1 percent for the year. While the company has reported that volume case unit growth in Europe, the Middle East, and Africa has risen 3 percent so far this year, and increased by 4 percent in the past quarter.

Coca-Cola has been relying on what is increasingly showing to be unsustainable pricing power. The company raised prices nearly 89 percent on many soda brand products since 2020, and new economic data shows that lower-income consumers are facing significant spending constraints that are impacting the beverage seller, in part because Coca-Cola is now selling many labels at historically high price levels. A 2-liter of Coke has gone from nearly $1.50 to almost $3.00 since 2019.

Coke's CFO Henrique Braun recently noted in December that the company is seeing a low-income consumer pullback, and this commentary is also consistent with what a number of companies, including McDonald's (MCD) and Walmart, have seen as well.The Fed also recently noted a significant increase in thrift spending and other data suggesting struggles of lower-income families and individuals. Credit card debt is also near record highs. The Congress's recent cuts to SNAP food stamp benefits and the possibility of these programs no longer being allowed to be used for soft drinks should concern investors as well.Five states recently enacted bans on individuals' ability to use SNAP benefits for candy and soft drinks.

The recent weakness in the dollar is also likely unsustainable. While predicting short-term forex moves is always tenuous, the US economy remains significantly stronger than its Asian or European peers. The dollar has also stabilized since June against most major currencies. US economic growth in the third quarter was 4.4 percent, which was notably above the Eurozone's 1.3 percent growth rate and Japan's economic contraction of 2.3 percent. China also recently saw the country's second-largest economic growth rate slow to a 3-year low of 4.5 percent on a year-to-year basis.

This is why the Coca-Cola company looks overvalued at24.21x expected forward earnings.While the beverage seller's 5-year average valuation is 24.83x forecasted forward earnings, growth expectations are more muted, and the higher cost operating environment that is likely to persistent, remains more challenging.

Analysts are projecting the beverage seller to grow earnings just 6-8 percent over the next few years, and these projections are also likely overly optimistic, since the company's stagnating volume numbers and lower-income consumers' struggles indicate management's pricing power should be significantly constrained moving forward.

All investment theses have risks, and the Coca-Cola company's revenues and cash flow remain stable, management is unlikely to reduce dividends or share buybacks. Trump's use of tariffs also remains unpredictable, further dollar weakness in the near term is certainly possible. The company is also seeing strength in higher-end water and other beverage brands such as Smartwater, Topo Chico, and Fairlife. This could help offset what will likely be continued struggles with lower-income consumers.

Coca-Cola is a very different company from the beverage seller that Warren Buffett originally invested in decades ago. The corporation is now pricing products at much higher levels, even adjusted for inflation, and while the beverage seller's portfolio brands are more diverse today, the company is still heavily dependent on a core Coca-Cola brand that has seen global market share levels of nearly 40 percent largely flatline over the last 3 years.Indeed, with lower-income consumers facing what will likely be continued adversity from higher prices, the beverage seller should continue to face mounting struggles to substantively grow earnings and revenue moving forward.

data.title: Nature's Sunshine: Recent Growth Shouldn't Be Extrapolated Far
data.description:
Nature’s Sunshine Products (NATR) has caught the market’s interest. The company’s growth and momentum recently accelerated to a great level in Q3. NATR's direct selling strategy has gained momentum in many markets with a number of nutritional products, showing up as great earnings momentum in the latest report. A number of strategic initiatives have worked. I believe that earnings growth shouldn’t be extrapolated, though, as NATR's business model comes with key risks.

The stock has risen by 66% in the past year, primarily reflecting a surge after great Q3 results.

A Brief Introduction to NATR

Founded in 1972, NATR manufactures and sells nutritional and personal care products. The company sells a portfolio of over 800 products, including health products related to general, cardiovascular and digestive health, personal care, and weight management. Most of NATR’s products are manufactured in the company’s Utah facility, but are sold on a global basis – NATR’s key markets include the U.S., Taiwan, South Korea, Japan, and China, but the company also has significant presence in Europe.

NATR utilizes a direct selling strategy; the company markets its products through “independent consultants”, which primarily receive commissions based on their own, and their sales organization’s, sales volume. The selling strategy can be characterized as a multi-level marketing "MLM" business model; NATR relies on people that buy their products to market them forward to friends and relatives, ultimately creating a multi-level sales funnel.

The company has competitors such as Herbalife (HLF), USANA Health Sciences (USNA) that I previously wrote an article on, and Nu Skin Enterprises (NUS) among others.

There’s Clear Financial Momentum, But Can It Last?

Enthusiasm around NATR has been based on recent financial momentum. The company has had quite a stable and moderate 4.0% revenue CAGR from 2015 forward, but the most recent Q3 report showed very sharp acceleration – revenue growth came in at 12.0% with total sales of $128.3 million, beating Wall Street’s consensus by $8.0 million.

Better revenue growth was immediately accretive to margins as well; adjusted EBITDA improved by 41.6% to $15.2 million, outpacing revenue growth significantly. Cost savings in logistics, sourcing, and other functions also helped NATR’s margin expansion.

The sudden Q3 growth acceleration was largely broad-based. Revenue grew by 8.9% in the U.S., by 33.2% in Japan, by 9.4% in South Korea, by 2.5% in Taiwan, and by 12.6% in other markets. All product categories except for weight management showed a good amount of growth. NATR’s CFO Shane Jones attributed better growth to strategic growth investments in the past two years in the Q3 earnings call, attracting new customers and improved engagement with existing ones. The addition of autoship programs and increased digital marketing investments have clearly succeeded.

The ultimate question is whether NATR’s accelerated financial momentum is sustainable over the longer term. I believe that there are a couple of factors pointing towards a likely growth slowdown.

Most notably, despite a guidance raise, the current $476-480 million 2025 revenue range only implies 2.9% mid-point growth for Q4. NATR doesn’t expect to maintain quite as fast momentum going forward, seeming to make the Q3 report a one-off success. NATR’s underlying momentum isn’t as fast as Q3 revenues suggest. Growth can vary quite noticeably on a quarterly basis; a part of NATR’s great Q3 growth was due to revenue timing in the Asia Pacific region, reversing in the upcoming report.

Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 is implied at $9.6-11.6 million, suggesting stable margins year-on-year.

Another key factor to consider is NATR’s business model. The MLM model can be inconsistent as its value proposition is often questioned. NATR has to pay a significant portion of sales, 30.7% in Q3, as sales incentives. Consumers effectively have to pay for such high marketing; NATR’s gross margin is incredibly high at 73.3% in Q3, significantly higher than a typical consumer staples company’s. A competitor with a more traditional marketing model could potentially offer similar products for much cheaper, questioning NATR’s long-term ability to generate high margins and grow.

The MLM model is also prone to significant flywheels as I outlined in a previous article on Medifast (MED) – sales can have large swings in either direction based on expansion of contraction of the multi-level distribution network. In NATR’s sector, Nu Skin’s recent years’ revenue decline reflects such a flywheel to the negative direction. I believe that NATS’ diversified global market presence makes the company quite resistant, but not immune, to such flywheels.

NATR previously experienced good growth during 2021, but revenues ultimately cooled down in 2022.

The company does note that it expects growth momentum to continue in 2026, for example expecting mid-single-digit growth in Europe in the year. Growth in digital channels, regional sales teams, and new products are aimed to grow NATR’s scale. There is clear reason for optimism after broad-based Q3 growth and strong margins, but I don’t believe that growth should be extrapolated far.

Not Much Is Priced In Even After NATR’s Surge

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for NATR stock. I expect revenue momentum to quickly slow down to 2% perpetual growth, assuming that long-term growth mainly reflects food inflation. I expect the adjusted EBITDA margin to stay stable from 2025 forward at 10%. I estimate that NATR’s CapEx will be relatively moderate going forward, making the company’s free cash flow healthy.

The estimates put NATR’s fair value at $32.0, suggesting 29% upside as of writing. Despite the stock’s recent surge, not much seems to be priced in. The stock is relatively attractively valued, but as the MLM business model comes with much higher uncertainty, I believe that current upside mostly represents a fair margin of safety.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.62%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume NATR to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.27% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance estimates NATR’s beta at 0.92. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 8.62%.

Takeaway

Optimism around NATR has been based on the company’s sudden growth acceleration and strong margins in Q3. Unfortunately, I believe that such growth shouldn’t be extrapolated – NATR’s Q4 guidance suggests a slowdown, and the MLM business model comes with high long-term uncertainty. The stock doesn’t price in much growth, but mostly for good reason in my opinion. I initiate NATR at a Hold rating.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Guidance Cools Down 2026 Recovery Expectations
data.description:
STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM) is on its path towards a recovery. The semiconductor giant’s important industrial and automotive end markets have remained volatile, but 2025 has shown gradual progress in an earnings recovery as the company’s new Q4 earnings report highlights. Long-term trends should aid STM in a recovery and in achieving growth. STM’s weak Q1 guidance did put a stop to too enthusiastic recovery expectations, though, sending the stock down by -5% as of writing – the company should continue to recover in 2026, but the outlook remains volatile.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous September 2024 article on the stock, titled “STMicroelectronics: From Tailwinds To Headwinds, To Likely Back”. The stock since lost -2% of its value, losing to the S&P 500’s 20% gain.

Setting the Scene: The Industry Backdrop Has Remained Challenging for STM

The industry backdrop still hasn’t been easy on STM in 2025. Two of the company’s key end markets, automotive and industrial, have remained weak. Better growth in communications equipment & computer peripherals “CECP”, and personal electronics haven’t stabilized the overall outlook.

Car registrations have been very subdued in the U.S., and while European car registrations have turned towards good growth in the latter half of the year, the regions’ car production has been very weak – S&P Global estimates light vehicle production to have weakened noticeably in North America in 2025, and from 2024 forward in Europe as Chinese EV production has pressured the markets’ key manufacturers. Additionally, STM’s key automotive OEM customer Tesla (TSLA) has reported a significant slowdown in vehicle sales that began in 2024, causing an especially hard impact on STM. Hyundai, another key customer, reported slow 0.1% growth in unit sales in 2025.

The weak production trend has combined with STM’s customers’ more cautious inventory management in especially the first half of 2025, making both automotive and industrial revenue extraordinarily weak while STM’s customers churn through excess inventory. End market weakness has continued to weaken STM’s earnings prospects going into Q4.

STM’s Gradual Recovery Continued in Q4

The Q4 report showed some progress in STM’s recovery as the company’s earnings have gradually improved from a bleak Q1’2025 performance. The topline improved by 4.5% sequentially and remained stable year-on-year at $3.33 billion, beating Wall Street’s consensus by $43 million. As STM’s revenue seasonality isn’t very strong, I believe that quarter-over-quarter comparisons matter the most in examining growth.

Sequential growth was driven by a number of end markets. Most notably, the CECP market showed rapid 23% sequential growth, driven by silicon and silicon carbine-based power solutions for AI and data center infrastructure. The pace picked up from already good 4% and 6% sequential growth in the previous two quarters. Projected 23% annual growth in data center infrastructure investments in the 2025-2030 period. The current AI CapEx cycle is boding well for STM; the CECP end market seems likely to generate great growth for STM in the next few years.

The key automotive and industrial end markets also showed stabilization at 3% and 5% sequential growth respectively. Design wins in automotive in both EVs and traditional vehicles, and in industrial automation and robotics stabilized the performance. It also seems that customers’ excess inventory is gradually normalizing, improving new orders. The Q4 end market performance also sent quite good signals regarding STM’s recovery.

The personal electronics end market showed a -2% sequential decline, but the Q4 performance doesn’t seem critical and is rather just quarterly fluctuation. STM expects personal electronics to show growth in 2026.

Better revenue is quick to also improve STM’s profitability as less capacity is left idle; the adjusted operating margin came in at 8.0% for Q4 with adjusted EBIT of $266 million, improving by 120 basis points sequentially. STM’s cost savings initiative has also helped stabilize profitability on an adjusted basis, meanwhile GAAP profitability slowed down temporarily due to related restructuring and impairment charges.

STM’s $0.11 adjusted EPS did miss Wall Street’s more ambitious consensus estimate by $0.18. The quarter’s gross margin was still quite weak at 35.2%, leaving more room for a recovery; STM has reported an average 40.0% gross margin during 2015-2024, and reported a much better 37.7% gross margin a year ago in Q4’2024. Total revenues were still quite subdued compared to most previous years, creating a need for more improvement, especially with STM’s current cost base.

A Weak Q1 Guidance Moderates 2026 Recovery Expectations

A weak Q1 guidance cools down expectations of a very fast recovery in 2026. STM expects the quarter’s revenues to come in at $3.04 billion, down by -8.7% sequentially plus or minus 350 basis points. While seasonality does typically make STM’s revenue slightly weaker for Q1, seasonality doesn’t explain the entirety of the weak revenue guidance. Slower sales again make STM’s margin outlook weaker, as the Q1 gross margin is guided at just 33.7% plus or minus 200 basis points.

Regardless, it does seem that STM is positioned to grow at least modestly in 2026 – STM notes better visibility going into 2026 than into 2025, and its confidence to be able to grow organically in 2026. While low automotive production seems likely to continue to cause pressure, likely reflected in the Q1 guidance, there are several positive factors driving revenue growth for the year. STM’s weak 2025 performance gives a low revenue level to compare to.

Both the automotive and industrial markets’ inventory position is gradually normalizing, resuming better orders. Industrial production has improved in the U.S. and Europe to positive levels in early 2025 already, likely to translate into better demand for STM. Design wins in automotive and industrial should drive incremental demand, and momentum in automotive ADAS programs and an improved market position in MCUs in industrial create a further boost. Over the long term, a growing share of EVs in passenger cars, data center investments, and other secular trends aid STM in achieving growth and returning margins closer to the long-term average.

The cycle does remain uncertain in many of STM’s end markets; a weak Q1 guidance clearly reflects macroeconomic volatility. I believe that good long-term secular trends ultimately matter more for investors that are willing to look past near-term uncertainty. A starting recovery in 2025, and STM’s faith in 2026 growth, set up a fair foundation even though a major change in the business cycle doesn’t seem likely to happen in 2026 yet given a weak Q1 guidance.

A Growth Recovery Is Already Priced In

I revised my discounted cash flow (DCF) model estimates for STM. I expect 2026 revenue growth to come in at 6% as the company’s 2025 revenue baseline is low, and that growth will accelerate to 10% in 2027 with improving end market demand. Afterwards, I expect growth to gradually slow down to 3% perpetual growth, implying a 2025-2035 CAGR of 7.0%.

Better revenues and pricing should recover STM’s margin level. I estimate the adjusted EBIT margin to rebound to 12% over the next few years, far from STM’s peak level but well above the company’s current earnings. The estimate assumes a gross margin rebound to STM’s historical average, and some further expansion from operating leverage in SG&A.

STM should convert after-tax earnings relatively well into free cash flow going forward. The company has invested significantly in the past few years, enabling growth without excessive further CapEx.

The estimates put STM’s fair value at $26.1, 9% below the stock price as of writing. STM stock still isn’t very attractively valued; an earnings recovery, and a good amount of long-term growth, are already priced in. Given remaining uncertainty in the end market outlook, I believe that it’s reasonable to wait for a better entry point.

The fair value estimate is down from $39.0 previously due to weaker revenue and margin expectations.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.73%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

As STM has a very healthy amount of net cash, I now don’t assume the company to leverage a noteworthy amount of debt over the long term. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.24% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. Yahoo Finance now estimates STM’s beta at 1.29. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 9.73%.

Takeaway

STM’s Q4 report showed progress in the company’s earnings recovery; the automotive and industrial end markets showed some stabilization, and CECP showed great growth. Margins improved but remain subdued. While stabilizing customer inventories and healthy end demand tailwinds aid an earnings recovery, STM’s weak Q1 guidance highlights that the recovery is still on volatile grounds in 2026. I believe that STM stock is roughly fairly valued, and remain at a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kevin Warsh Nominated To Be Next Fed Chair, What It Means For Markets
data.description:
Market observers have been eagerly awaiting President Trump’s next pick for Federal Reserve ("Fed") Chairman. In 2018, he elevated current Chairman, Jerome Powell, to the role after declining to renominate then Chair, Janet Yellen, for a second term.

Historically, there has been the underlying presumption that President Trump seeks loyalty and expects those he elevates to bend to his will. For the Fed specifically, this largely entails adjusting interest rates to the whims of his political pressure.

Though a Trump appointee, Powell has always maintained an independent posture through his entire term, even in the face of a vehement pressure campaign. To little surprise, then, Trump soured on Powell through his term. With his second chance, the expectation was that Trump’s next pick would be a more malleable candidate.

In a previous Seeking Alpha (“SA”) Asks Roundtable, I had provided my opinion that Kevin Hassett would be the eventual pick. My justification at the time was primarily due to the sentiment and commentary at the time surrounding his fit for the mold.

Today, the pick is in, and it is a Kevin, but not the one that I had been expecting previously. Trump’s pick of Kevin Warsh to lead the Fed may have important implications for the future trajectory of interest rates and for the broader market performance, especially in the second half of the calendar year.

With all three indexes trading at their highs, including the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), S&P 500 (SPY), and Nasdaq Composite (NDX), markets are more exposed to risks, and even minor developments could send one or all of the indexes lower. I ultimately believe a less independent Fed will be negative for markets due to the increased volatility.

Here’s everything you need to know about President Trump’s next Fed pick.

Who Is Kevin Warsh?

Kevin Warsh is no stranger to financial markets or monetary policymaking, having served as a Fed Governor from 2006 to 2011 after a career on Wall Street and in the George W. Bush administration. His tenure placed him at the center of the Global Financial Crisis ("GFS"), where he played a behind-the-scenes role in Washington’s extraordinary efforts to stabilize the banking system.

Since leaving the Fed, Warsh has remained deeply engaged in economic policy debates, positioning himself as one of the central bank’s most vocal critics.

A case in point was on full display in a November 2025 Wall Street Journal op-ed. In my view, the piece offered a revealing window into how Warsh views the Fed’s role. In his write-up, Warsh sharply criticized Fed leadership for what he described as policy inertia, regulatory overreach, and a failure to adapt to a rapidly evolving economy.

He further argued that AI and American innovation are powerful disinflationary forces that the Fed is underappreciating, while blaming persistent inflation on excessive government spending and monetary expansion rather than wage growth.

Warsh also called for aggressive balance-sheet reduction, regulatory reform, and a pivot toward policies that favor households and small businesses over Wall Street.

Collectively, I believe his most recent commentary in the WSJ Op-Ed reflects a deep skepticism toward the Fed’s current framework and a willingness to fundamentally reshape the institution. These are traits that now place him squarely in position to lead it.

Why Did President Trump Nominate Kevin Warsh As Next Fed Chair?

The Fed chair nomination is arguably the most consequential personnel decision President Trump will make for the remainder of his term, in my view. After souring quickly on Jerome Powell, Trump has long signaled regret for not selecting Kevin Warsh in 2017, even publicly acknowledging in 2020 that he wished Warsh had been “more forceful” in pursuing the role. This time around, Trump appears determined not to repeat that mistake.

Warsh has also aligned himself more closely with Trump’s policy preferences, including publicly calling for faster rate cuts, endorsing tariff policies he once opposed, and advocating for what he has termed a “regime change” at the Fed. This could be seen in his commentary in his November WSJ Op-Ed.

The rhetoric likely made him the preferred candidate over Kevin Hassett, whom I previously viewed as the front-runner. Hassett’s deep entrenchment within the White House economic team and Trump’s stated desire to keep him in place probably weakened his candidacy, in my view.

That said, the appointment is not yet finalized. Warsh must still clear Senate confirmation, a process complicated by an ongoing Justice Department probe into the Fed that has already drawn political resistance. Even so, Trump’s selection of Warsh likely signals a clear desire to exert greater influence over the central bank and reshape its policy direction.

What Does The Kevin Warsh Pick Mean For Fed Policy?

Possible independence issues aside, it must be emphasized that Kevin Warsh is highly credible and he does have institutional experience to bring back to the Fed.

Having previously served as a Fed governor during the GFS, Warsh understands both the operational complexities of monetary policy and the political pressures that accompany it. His background suggests a steady hand at the helm, even as markets anticipate a more accommodative policy stance once he assumes office.

Interest-rate expectations have already been reflecting the transition. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, the probability of a rate cut remains below 50% prior to May, but those odds are above the majority immediately after Powell's term ends. This supports investor expectations that Warsh will pursue a looser policy path.

In a summary of thoughts prepared by the WSJ, it appears economists remain divided on what that means in practice. Joseph Brusuelas of RSM, for example, characterized Warsh as traditionally hawkish, while Stephen Brown of Capital Economics argued Warsh may be one of the better outcomes for investors, citing his credibility and intellectual consistency. Others, including Guy Berger of Guild and Bas van Geffen of Rabobank, believe Warsh’s tone has softened, suggesting his past hawkish reputation may give way to a more flexible approach.

Final Thoughts On The Kevin Warsh Pick

My belief is that Kevin Warsh brings unquestioned credibility and deep institutional knowledge back to the Fed.

In my view, his prior experience as a Fed governor during the GFS provides him with a rare perspective on monetary policy under extreme conditions, and it is entirely possible that, like Powell, Warsh ultimately emerges as a pragmatic, straight-shooting central banker rather than the political instrument many fear. Powell himself was initially viewed as a Trump loyalist yet consistently defended the Fed’s independence once in office.

That said, this term presents a very different political environment. President Trump appears far more emboldened, and the pressure campaign exerted on Powell offers a preview of what likely awaits Warsh. I believe that even a well-intentioned chair may find it increasingly difficult to resist political influence, particularly if markets falter or growth slows. In that environment, the risk of a less independent Fed becomes materially higher.

From a market perspective, I view any loss of independence as a net negative. It can’t be stressed enough that central bank credibility is foundational to financial stability, and erosion of that trust tends to amplify volatility. With equity valuations already stretched, markets are increasingly sensitive to policy missteps, in my view.

Finally, it's important to remember that Warsh’s nomination is not yet a sealed deal. Senate confirmation remains uncertain and could prove complicated. Until that process is resolved, policy uncertainty is likely to remain elevated.
